name: Canada
summary: English-speaking with a clear MSc->PGWP->Express Entry->PR->fast 3-year citizenship route, but 2024-2026 study caps, low study-permit approval from Bangladesh, dormant STEM draws and tightened spousal work permits add real friction for a two-engineer couple.

job-market.summary: Deep English-speaking demand for software/AI talent, but 2025-26 is 'precision hiring' with layoffs and newcomer friction.
job-market.reasoning: Toronto, Vancouver, Montreal and Waterloo host large tech sectors and Robert Half reports ~48% of IT managers expanding teams in 2026 with AI/ML, cloud and data skills scarce; however hiring is selective amid recent layoffs, so two newcomers without Canadian experience face real friction before landing roles.
evidence: Robert Half 2026: ~48% of Canadian IT managers plan to grow teams; specialized AI/cloud talent scarce
GTA software developer postings ~8,700/yr; AI mentioned in ~37% of software postings (late 2025)
Market shifted to selective 'precision hiring' amid 2025-26 tech layoffs

links.title: Robert Half 2026 IT salary & hiring guide (Canada)

visa-access.summary: Biometrics in Dhaka, but no SDS, low ~32% study approval, heavy financial bar; meds assessed in New Delhi.
visa-access.reasoning: Bangladeshis apply via the regular stream (SDS ended Nov 2024) with biometrics at VFS Dhaka/Chattogram/Sylhet, but the 2025 national study-permit approval rate fell to about 32% and 'financial insufficiency' is the top Dhaka refusal reason; the cost-of-living bar is ~CAD 22,895 plus tuition each and medical results route through IRCC's New Delhi office.
evidence: SDS closed 8 Nov 2024 - all Bangladeshi applicants use the regular stream
2025 study-permit approval rate ~32% (refusals ~52% in 2024); financial insufficiency a top Dhaka refusal reason
Proof-of-funds ~CAD 22,895 + tuition per applicant; biometrics in Dhaka, medicals via New Delhi RMO

citizenship.summary: Citizenship after 1,095 days (3 of 5 yrs); only CLB 4 language; dual citizenship fully allowed.
citizenship.reasoning: Among the fastest routes for this couple: physically present 1,095 days in the 5 years before applying (min 730 as PR), modest CLB 4 language test, and Canada is one of the most dual-citizenship-friendly nations; Bill C-3 (2026) further liberalises citizenship by descent.
evidence: 1,095 days physical presence in 5 years (min 730 as PR)
Language only CLB 4 for ages 18-54; routine processing ~12-14 months
Dual citizenship permitted; Bill C-3 expands descent rules (2026)

post-study-work.summary: Master's grads get a 3-year PGWP, field-of-study exempt; CLB 7 language proof required.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of a university master's program receive a PGWP of up to 3 years regardless of program length (min 8 months) and are exempt from the field-of-study requirement, letting both engineers work openly and switch employers; since Nov 2024 they must show CLB 7 (easy for IT professionals).
evidence: Master's grads: up to 3-year open PGWP regardless of program length
Degree grads exempt from PGWP field-of-study list (frozen for 2026)
CLB 7 language proof required for university grads (since 1 Nov 2024)

spouse-family.summary: Both can accompany; spousal open work permit survives for MSc 16+ months but 2025-26 rules narrowed it.
spouse-family.reasoning: From 21 Jan 2025 a spousal open work permit (SOWP) is limited to spouses of master's programs of at least 16 months, doctoral or listed professional programs, so most thesis MSc qualify; in the work phase the principal must hold a skilled job (TEER 0/1 or select 2/3) with 16+ months left, and a March 2026 rule refuses SOWPs in the student's final term - so both can work but with conditions, not unconditionally.
evidence: SOWP for students restricted to master's >=16 months / doctoral / professional programs (21 Jan 2025)
Work-phase SOWP requires principal in skilled occupation with 16+ months authorization remaining
March 2026: SOWP refused if student is in final academic term

links.title: IRCC open work permits for spouses (eligibility)
IRCC work permit for spouses of students

msc-study.summary: Abundant English MSc; high coursework tuition (~CAD 35k/yr) though research MSc often funded; 24h/wk work.
msc-study.reasoning: Broad English-taught master's catalogue at strong universities (e.g. UofT MSc ~CAD 34,900 in 2025-26); thesis/research MSc commonly carry TA/RA stipends and awards (CGS-M, Vanier CAD 50k), graduate students are exempt from the study-permit cap, and students may work off-campus up to 24 hours/week with the spouse able to accompany.
evidence: Wide English-taught MSc catalogue; coursework tuition ~CAD 35,000/yr (UofT 2025-26)
Research MSc often funded via TA/RA + CGS-M / Vanier (CAD 50k) awards
Off-campus work up to 24 hrs/week; graduate students exempt from study-permit cap

links.title: University of Toronto CS graduate funding & tuition

pr-pathway.summary: Clear ~3.5-4yr study->PGWP->CEC route, but CEC CRS ~507-511 and STEM draws dormant since Apr 2024.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Express Entry (CEC after 1 yr skilled Canadian work) is a predictable route taking roughly 3.5-4 years from study start, but 2026 CEC cut-offs sit at a high 507-511 and dedicated STEM category draws have been dormant since April 2024; French at CLB 7 unlocks far lower category cut-offs (~336-409), so PR is attainable but competitive without French.
evidence: Study->PGWP->CEC pathway ~3.5-4 years; EE PR processing ~6-8 months
2026 CEC CRS cut-offs ~507-511; STEM category draws dormant since April 2024
French CLB 7 category draws cut as low as ~336-409 CRS in 2026

income-cost.summary: Strong dual tech salaries (CAD 85-220k each) but Toronto/Vancouver housing erodes savings.
income-cost.reasoning: Software roles in Toronto pay ~CAD 85-120k entry to 155-220k senior and ~15% above national average, so a two-engineer household earns well; however a downtown 1-bed rents ~CAD 2,650/month and Toronto cost of living runs ~34% above the national average, so net savings are good but not exceptional in the big metros.
evidence: GTA software engineer total comp ~CAD 85-120k entry, 155-220k senior
Downtown Toronto 1-bed rent ~CAD 2,650/month; cost of living ~34% above national average
Dual income materially improves household savings vs single earner

healthcare.summary: Universal and free for PR/citizens, high quality, but long waits and family-doctor shortage.
healthcare.reasoning: Provincial plans cover permanent residents and citizens (waiting period capped at 3 months, often waived) at no point-of-care cost and quality is high; however the median wait for treatment hit ~28.6 weeks in 2025-26 and there is a family-doctor shortage, and international students must buy provincial/private student insurance during study.
evidence: Universal provincial coverage for PR/citizens; <=3-month wait period, often waived
Median wait for treatment ~28.6 weeks (2025-26); family-doctor shortage
International students need provincial/private student health insurance

links.title: Government of Canada - access universal health care

culture-language.summary: English daily and official multiculturalism; no language barrier for PR or citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: English (and French in Quebec) is the working language, Canada has official multiculturalism and a large immigrant population, and PR/citizenship language thresholds (CLB 7 / CLB 4) are easily met by IT professionals - so day-to-day integration is straightforward.
evidence: English working language outside Quebec; official multiculturalism policy
Large immigrant share of population eases integration
No additional language barrier for English-speaking PR/citizenship applicants

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 13th) and peaceful (GPI 14th); strong immigrant protections.
safety-law.reasoning: Canada ranks 13th of 143 on the 2025 WJP Rule of Law Index (0.79) and 14th on the 2025 Global Peace Index with Charter protections and broadly safe cities; violent crime has edged up over the decade, slightly tempering an otherwise strong score.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 13th of 143 (score 0.79)
Global Peace Index 2025: 14th of 163; Crime Severity Index down ~4% in 2024
Charter rights and strong protections for immigrants

politics.summary: Stable democracy but sharp 2024-26 immigration cuts and rising public sentiment add volatility.
politics.reasoning: Institutions are stable, but the 2026-2028 plan cut PR targets to 380,000, halved new study-permit allocations (~155,000) and slashed temporary residents amid waning public support for immigration; graduate students are exempted from the study cap, which softens the impact for this couple but the policy direction is tightening.
evidence: 2026-2028 plan: PR target 380k (from 483k in 2024); new study permits ~155k (-49%)
Temporary residents cut from 673,650 (2025) toward 385,000 (2026); rising anti-immigration sentiment
Graduate (master's/PhD) students exempted from the study-permit cap

tax.summary: Moderate burden: ~43% marginal at CAD 100k in Ontario; OECD tax wedge ~32% (26th lowest).
tax.reasoning: Combined federal-provincial marginal rate is ~43% at CAD 100k in Ontario (top combined ~53.5%), but the OECD single-worker tax wedge is ~32.0% - the 26th lowest of 38 OECD countries - so the effective burden on tech salaries is moderate rather than punishing, and the lowest federal rate dropped to 14% for 2026.
evidence: Ontario combined marginal rate ~43% at CAD 100k; top combined ~53.5%
OECD single-worker tax wedge ~32.0% (2024) - 26th lowest of 38 OECD members
Lowest federal PIT rate cut to 14% for 2026

links.title: Canada Revenue Agency individual tax rates

muslim-diaspora.summary: Large Muslim (~1.8M) and Bangladeshi (~100k) communities; mosques and halal ubiquitous in cities.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Canada has ~1.78M Muslims (4.9%, 2021), with the GTA hosting 626,000+ Muslims and 100+ mosques, and roughly 100,000 Bangladeshi-origin Canadians concentrated in Toronto - so halal food, mosques and a familiar South-Asian community are readily available with comparatively low hostility.
evidence: ~1.78M Muslims in Canada (4.9%, 2021); 626,000+ in the GTA with 100+ Toronto mosques
~100,000 Bangladeshi-origin Canadians, ~26,650 in the City of Toronto (2021)
South Asians are the largest racialized Muslim group (37.6%); halal widely available

links.title: Statistics Canada - religion and ethnocultural data
